description: Mathematical expression showing the general form of the policy gradient theorem, expressing the gradient of the objective as an expectation over states and actions of the score function times Q-values, with multiple equivalent formulations listed.
teaching_value: Consolidates multiple equivalent forms of the policy gradient into one reference, helping students see how different baseline choices (Q, advantage, reward-to-go) all derive from the same theorem while understanding the bias-variance tradeoff.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the policy gradient theorem, before discussing specific algorithms like REINFORCE or A3C, to establish the theoretical foundation all subsequent methods build upon.
suggested_caption: General form of policy gradient theorem with equivalent formulations. (Schulman et al., 2016)

description: Side-by-side architecture diagram comparing A3C (left) and A2C (right). A3C shows multiple async worker agents each independently pushing gradients to a global network at different times. A2C shows a coordinator synchronizing all parallel actors before updating the global network.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the key architectural difference between A3C and A2C — asynchronous vs. synchronous gradient updates — making it easy to understand why A2C can be more stable and GPU-efficient despite being simpler.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from A3C to A2C to highlight the single design change (synchronization) and its implications for training stability and hardware utilization.
suggested_caption: A3C uses async independent agents; A2C uses a synchronizing coordinator.

description: Pseudocode for the DDPG algorithm, detailing initialization of critic and actor networks with random weights, replay buffer setup, and the main loop covering action selection with exploration noise, experience storage, mini-batch sampling, target computation, critic/actor updates, and soft target network updates.
teaching_value: Provides a step-by-step implementation blueprint for DDPG, making abstract concepts like soft target updates (τ), experience replay, and deterministic policy gradients concrete and actionable for students.
when_to_show: Show when teaching DDPG implementation details, especially to contrast soft target updates (τ ≪ 1) with DQN's frozen target network approach.
suggested_caption: DDPG algorithm with soft target updates and experience replay. (Lillicrap et al., 2015)

description: Pseudocode for the D4PG algorithm, showing distributed actor setup with K parallel actors feeding a shared replay buffer, distributional critic updates using categorical distributions, N-step TD targets, and priority-based sampling.
teaching_value: Illustrates how D4PG extends DDPG with four concrete improvements (distributional critic, N-step returns, distributed actors, prioritized replay) in a single unified algorithm, helping students understand each modification's role.
when_to_show: Show after explaining DDPG to highlight the specific algorithmic additions D4PG makes, particularly when discussing distributional RL or scalable training.
suggested_caption: D4PG extends DDPG with distributional critics and parallel actors. (Barth-Maron et al., 2018)

description: Architecture diagram of MADDPG showing multiple agents, each with its own actor (taking local observations) and a centralized critic (taking all agents' observations and actions). Arrows illustrate the flow of local observations to actors and global information to critics during training.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the centralized training / decentralized execution paradigm central to MADDPG, helping students understand how a shared critic with global information stabilizes training in non-stationary multi-agent environments.
when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-agent RL challenges, specifically to explain how centralized critics resolve the non-stationarity problem that arises when multiple agents learn simultaneously.
suggested_caption: MADDPG: centralized critics with global info, decentralized actors. (Lowe et al., 2017)

description: Pseudocode or loss function equations for PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization), showing the clipped surrogate objective, value function loss, and entropy bonus combined into a single loss expression.
teaching_value: Makes the PPO objective concrete by showing exactly how the clipped ratio, value loss, and entropy bonus are combined, helping students understand the practical implementation of trust-region-style constraints without solving a constrained optimization problem.
when_to_show: Show when teaching PPO's loss function, particularly to contrast with TRPO's KL-constrained optimization and explain why PPO is simpler to implement.
suggested_caption: PPO combines clipped policy ratio, value loss, and entropy bonus. (Cobbe et al., 2020)

description: Pseudocode for the PPG (Phasic Policy Gradient) algorithm, showing two alternating training phases: a policy phase updating the policy network with PPO, and an auxiliary phase updating the value network with distillation from the policy network using a behavioral cloning loss.
teaching_value: Clarifies how PPG separates policy and value optimization into distinct phases to avoid conflicting gradients, making the architectural innovation concrete and showing how the β_clone hyperparameter controls policy drift during auxiliary updates.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the tradeoffs of shared vs. separate policy/value networks, after introducing PPO, to illustrate how PPG resolves the parameter-sharing conflict.
suggested_caption: PPG alternates policy and auxiliary phases to separate network objectives. (Cobbe et al., 2020)

description: Bar chart or line plot comparing mean normalized performance of PPG versus PPO on the Procgen benchmark across multiple game environments, showing PPG achieving higher sample efficiency.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that PPG's architectural separation of policy and value training phases leads to measurable performance gains, grounding the theoretical motivation in concrete benchmark results.
when_to_show: Show after explaining PPG's algorithm to validate the claim that separating training phases improves sample efficiency, and to motivate why architectural choices in policy gradient methods matter.
suggested_caption: PPG outperforms PPO in mean normalized score on Procgen. (Cobbe et al., 2020)

description: Pseudocode for the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm, detailing initialization of policy, Q-function, and value networks, the main loop with environment interaction, replay buffer updates, and gradient steps for value, Q-function, and policy networks using entropy-regularized objectives.
teaching_value: Shows how SAC implements maximum entropy RL in practice, making the interplay between the soft value function, Q-function, and policy updates concrete, and highlighting the role of the temperature parameter α in balancing reward and entropy.
when_to_show: Show when teaching SAC to connect the theoretical maximum entropy objective to its practical implementation, especially when contrasting with DDPG's deterministic policy.
suggested_caption: SAC algorithm with entropy-regularized policy and soft value updates. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)

description: Pseudocode for SAC with automatically adjusted temperature, adding a gradient-based update rule for the temperature parameter α that minimizes a temperature loss objective targeting a desired minimum entropy threshold.
teaching_value: Shows how the brittle manual temperature tuning in SAC is replaced by an automatic dual optimization procedure, helping students understand how constrained optimization (Lagrangian relaxation) can automate hyperparameter selection in RL.
when_to_show: Show after the basic SAC algorithm when discussing its brittleness to temperature, to demonstrate how automatic adjustment resolves this limitation and makes SAC more robust across tasks.
suggested_caption: SAC with automatic temperature adjustment via dual gradient descent. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)

description: Pseudocode algorithm box for TD3 (Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient), detailing initialization, actor-critic update steps, target policy smoothing with clipped noise, delayed policy updates, and soft target network updates.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete, step-by-step procedural view of TD3's three key innovations — clipped double Q-learning, delayed policy updates, and target policy smoothing — showing exactly where each mechanism appears in the training loop.
when_to_show: Show after explaining each of TD3's three improvements individually; use the algorithm box to tie them together and let students locate each innovation in the pseudocode.
suggested_caption: TD3 algorithm with target smoothing and delayed updates. (Fujimoto et al., 2018)

description: Architecture diagram of IMPALA showing multiple parallel actor machines generating experience trajectories and sending them to a central learner, which updates policy and value function parameters and periodically broadcasts updated weights back to actors.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the decoupled acting-learning architecture of IMPALA, making it easy to see why a policy lag exists between actors and the learner and why off-policy correction (V-trace) is necessary.
when_to_show: Introduce when explaining why IMPALA needs V-trace; use the diagram to point out the policy gap created by decoupled actors and the central learner.
suggested_caption: IMPALA's decoupled actor-learner architecture enabling high-throughput RL training.
